text: The surgeon identifies a right indirect inguinal hernia and begins dissection at the medial umbilical ligament, progressing down to the supravesicular space. The dissection crosses into the preperitoneal plane, which is clearly visible and well-established, extending laterally to Bogros' space. The tissue planes separate smoothly without fusion.

The surgeon carefully dissects the lateral flap, pushing the fat laterally to protect the nerves. The dissection continues on the anterior portion, staying close to the hernia sac, and extends down to Cooper's ligament and the pubic tubercle, carrying the dissection medially beyond the symphysis pubis.

The surgeon uses cautery with gentle retraction to manage bleeding while working on the hernia sac. An oblique angle is employed to buzz directly on the hernia sac, pushing surrounding fat away. The surgeon navigates close to the epigastric vessels, ensuring the fat is pushed down to maintain clear visibility of the surgical field.

The surgeon carefully pulls down the chord lipoma, teasing it away from the chord structures while avoiding dissection into the retroperitoneum. Fatty structures are cut away, maintaining proximity to the chord structures near the deep internal ring.

The vas deferens and spermatic cord vessels come into view, with some fatty tissue visible around the cord. The surgeon stays medial to the vas, ensuring safety while separating creeping fat from the femoral space. The fat is taken down to the obturator fossa, where the obturator plug is typically removed.

The surgeon extends the dissection beyond the symphysis pubis, ensuring the flap is positioned with all fat pushed laterally. The flap is sufficiently advanced, and an extra-large Bard 3D Max mesh is prepared for placement.
The surgeon performs a typical flap closure using a V-lock suture, securing the tissue layers in place.
